title: RuneQuest
resolution_core: d100 roll-under skill with specials/criticals at fractions of skill (BRP)

vector_explanations.resolution.core_mechanic.roll_under_perc: Every skill is a percentile rating; you roll d100 and succeed by rolling equal to or under the skill, the mechanic that founded the Basic Roleplaying lineage.
vector_explanations.resolution.core_mechanic.opposed_rolls: The resistance table pits active characteristic against passive characteristic (e.g. POW vs POW for spells), giving a percentile chance shifted 5% per point of difference.
vector_explanations.resolution.degrees.critical_success: Rolling one-fifth of skill is a special (impaling/slashing effects); one-twentieth is a critical that ignores armor, so high skill widens the special and critical bands.
vector_explanations.resolution.degrees.fumble_tables: Rolls of 96-00 (depending on skill) fumble, sending the attacker to weapon fumble tables that can drop, break, or bury a weapon in an ally.
vector_explanations.resolution.modifiers.situational_modifie: GMs apply flat percentile bonuses or penalties for darkness, cover, and task difficulty, shifting the roll-under target before the d100 is thrown.
vector_explanations.character.skills.percentile_improvement_: Skills successfully used in play earn an experience check; between adventures you roll to improve each checked skill, learning by doing rather than by XP spend.
vector_explanations.character.attributes.rolled_stats: The seven characteristics (STR, CON, SIZ, INT, POW, DEX, CHA) are rolled on 3d6 (2d6+6 for some), and everything else derives from them.
vector_explanations.character.attributes.derived_attributes: Hit points come from CON and SIZ, damage bonus from STR+SIZ, spirit magic capacity from INT/POW, and defense from DEX, all computed off the rolled characteristics.
vector_explanations.combat.melee.strike_ranks: Combat actions resolve in strike rank order within the round, computed from SIZ, DEX, and weapon length, so long spears strike before daggers.
vector_explanations.combat.melee.hit_locations: Every successful melee blow rolls d20 on a hit location table (legs, abdomen, chest, arms, head), and armor and damage are applied to that location.
vector_explanations.combat.melee.percentage_opposed: Attack and parry are separate percentile skill rolls; a successful parry blocks damage up to the parrying weapon or shield's points, making melee a d100 duel.
vector_explanations.combat.wounds.hit_locations: Each body location carries its own hit point total; a location reduced to zero is disabled and one taken to negatives can be maimed or severed.
vector_explanations.combat.wounds.hit_points: Characters track total hit points derived from CON and SIZ alongside per-location points; reaching zero total means collapse and impending death.
vector_explanations.combat.wounds.armor_as_soak: Armor provides absorption points per location that subtract from each wound before it reaches hit points; criticals bypass armor entirely.
vector_explanations.character.lifecycle.death_and_dying: Zero total hit points or a destroyed head/chest location kills; the game expects lethal fights, with healing magic races to stop death within melee rounds.
vector_explanations.equipment.gear.armor_types: Armor is bought by piece and material (soft leather through plate), each type giving specific absorption points and encumbrance per covered location.
vector_explanations.magic.structure.runes_glyphs: Glorantha's cosmology is organized around Runes (Death, Life, Fire...); cults are defined by the Runes they command and rune magic expresses them mechanically.
vector_explanations.simulation.magic.rune_spells: Rune magic is gained by sacrificing POW to a cult's god, granting potent spells such as Shield or Sunspear tied to the deity's Runes.
vector_explanations.simulation.magic.spirit_magic: Battle magic (spirit magic) spells like Bladesharp and Healing are learned from shamans and cast by anyone, powered by magic points.
vector_explanations.magic.acquisition.cult_initiation: Joining a cult as initiate or Rune level is the primary path to magic: initiation grants access to rune spells, cult spirit magic, and cult skills training.
vector_explanations.magic.casting.mana_points: Casting spends magic points drawn from POW; a caster reduced to zero magic points falls unconscious, and points regenerate over hours.
vector_explanations.magic.casting.skill_roll_casting: Spirit magic requires a POWx5 percentile roll to take effect, so casting is itself a fallible skill-style test rather than automatic.
vector_explanations.magic.divine.miracles: Rune spells work as near-guaranteed divine miracles from the god, regained through worship rather than rest, and scaled by the caster's standing in the cult.
vector_explanations.downtime.activities.worship_ceremonies: Holy day worship ceremonies at temples are how initiates renew rune magic and maintain cult standing, anchoring the campaign calendar to the sacred year.
vector_explanations.downtime.activities.training_advancement: Between adventures characters pay guilds and cults for weeks of training to raise skills or research, the deliberate alternative to improvement-by-use checks.

title: Pendragon
resolution_core: d20 roll-under skill/trait/passion; opposed rolls compare successful results

vector_explanations.resolution.core_mechanic.roll_under_attr: All resolution is a d20 rolled against a skill, trait, or passion rated 1-20; rolling equal to or under succeeds, and rolling the value exactly is a critical.
vector_explanations.resolution.core_mechanic.opposed_rolls: Opposed resolution (sword vs sword, Awareness vs stealth) has both sides roll d20 under their values; the higher successful roll wins, criticals beat ordinary successes.
vector_explanations.resolution.degrees.critical_success: Rolling your exact skill number is a critical: in combat it doubles damage dice, and in trait or passion rolls it can grant experience checks and glory.
vector_explanations.resolution.degrees.critical_failure: A natural 20 (when skill is under 20) is a fumble with fixed consequences per system, such as dropping a weapon or a passion fumble driving the knight to shame.
vector_explanations.character.identity.paired_personality_tr: Knights carry thirteen opposed trait pairs (Chaste/Lustful, Energetic/Lazy, Merciful/Cruel) each summing to 20; the GM can demand trait rolls that override player intent, and famous traits (16+) earn glory.
vector_explanations.social.influence.passions: Passions such as Loyalty (Lord), Love (Family), Hospitality, and Hate (Saxons) are rated 1-20+, rolled to inspire deeds and quantifying what the knight cares about.
vector_explanations.mental.composure.passion_inspiration_ris: Invoking a passion before a deed can make the knight Inspired (+10 or criticals on a skill) but a failed passion roll inflicts melancholy and a fumble can bring madness.
vector_explanations.character.identity.honor_glory_status: Honor is itself a passion that can be lost through dishonorable acts; losing all honor removes the character from play, and knighthood imposes duties enforced by trait rolls.
vector_explanations.social.reputation.fame_renown: Glory is the score of the game: earned for battles, tournaments, traits, passions, and manor splendor, tallied every Winter Phase, and granting ability points at each 1,000.
vector_explanations.campaign.continuity.generational_play: The Great Pendragon Campaign spans roughly 485-566 AD; players are expected to play a knight, then his heir, then the heir's heir across the whole Arthurian arc.
vector_explanations.character.lifecycle.generational_play: Winter Phase childbirth and family tables generate heirs; when a knight dies or retires, his son inherits the manor and a portion of the father's glory to continue the dynasty.
vector_explanations.character.lifecycle.aging: From age 35 knights roll yearly on the aging table in the Winter Phase, losing points from randomly determined statistics until infirmity forces retirement.
vector_explanations.character.lifecycle.legacy_inheritance: Heirs inherit the manor, equipment, family passions, and one-tenth of the deceased knight's glory, making dynasty-building the campaign's long game.
vector_explanations.downtime.structure.downtime_phases: Every year of play closes with a structured Winter Phase: solo scenarios, experience checks, training, aging, economics, family rolls, and horse survival in fixed steps.
vector_explanations.downtime.activities.family_events: The Winter Phase family events table brings marriages, births, deaths, and scandals to the knight's kin, generating heirs and story hooks annually.
vector_explanations.downtime.activities.training_advancement: Each Winter Phase a knight may train to add a point to a skill (up to 15), trait, or passion, or roll experience checks earned in play to improve further.
vector_explanations.campaign.management.campaign_calendars: Play advances one adventure per game year against a fixed 80-year Arthurian chronology, with published year-by-year events from Uther's reign to Camlann.
vector_explanations.logistics.maintenance.home_life: Knights hold a manor whose harvests, hospitality, and upkeep are resolved each winter; a poorly maintained manor degrades the knight's standard of living and glory.
vector_explanations.social.relationships.romance_rules: Courtly love has explicit rules: an Amor passion toward a lady is cultivated through stages of courtship and grants inspiration, distinct from the Love (spouse) passion.
vector_explanations.combat.wounds.hit_points: Hit points equal SIZ plus CON; damage accumulates against them, with unconsciousness and death thresholds and a Major Wound state changing the stakes of every blow.
vector_explanations.combat.wounds.massive_damage: Any single blow equal to or exceeding CON is a Major Wound, dropping the knight prone with lasting debilitation until fully healed and risking death or maiming.
vector_explanations.combat.wounds.armor_as_soak: Chainmail (10 points) and later plate soak damage from every hit, plus 6 for a shield on a successful defense, so armor quality tracks the campaign's era.
vector_explanations.downtime.recovery.natural_healing_rates: Wounded knights recover a fixed healing rate in hit points per week of rest, with First Aid restoring some immediately and Chirurgery improving the weekly rate.
vector_explanations.vehicles.travel.mount_riding: The charger is core equipment: Horsemanship rolls govern combat from the saddle, horses have their own stats and damage, and Winter Phase checks decide their survival.
vector_explanations.combat.maneuvers.charge: A mounted lance charge uses the Lance skill and the horse's damage bonus, dealing devastating doubled-dice hits and defining tournament jousts and battlefield shock.

title: Burning Wheel Gold
resolution_core: d6 dice pool; each die at or above the shade threshold (4+ Black) is a success versus an Obstacle

vector_explanations.resolution.core_mechanic.dice_pool_succe: Every test rolls a pool of d6s equal to the governing skill or stat; each die at or above the shade threshold (4+ for Black shade, 3+ for Grey) is a success, and you need successes equal to the Obstacle.
vector_explanations.resolution.target_numbers.success_thresh: Difficulty is set as an Obstacle number — the count of successes the task demands — so the GM sizes challenges by how many hits are required rather than by a per-die target number.
vector_explanations.resolution.target_numbers.contested_tn: Directly opposed actions are Versus tests where both sides roll and compare successes, and a Bloody Versus resolves a single lethal exchange of blows by the same comparison.
vector_explanations.resolution.target_numbers.extended_tests: Linked tests chain rolls so a success feeds a bonus die into the next, and set-piece conflicts like Fight! and the Duel of Wits accumulate results across several scripted volleys.
vector_explanations.resolution.degrees.fail_forward: The 'say yes or roll the dice' rule brings out dice only when failure is interesting, so a failed roll imposes the GM's stated consequence or twist instead of a dead stop.
vector_explanations.resolution.modifiers.help_dice: Allies contribute help dice and the acting character may add dice from Fields of Related Knowledge (FoRKs), pooling relevant expertise into a single roll at shared risk.
vector_explanations.resolution.metacurrency.hero_points: Artha — Fate, Persona, and Deeds points earned by playing your Beliefs and Instincts — is spent to reroll sixes, add dice, or double results, and accumulated spending fuels trait and skill epiphanies.
vector_explanations.character.character_creation.lifepath: Characters are built by 'burning' a lifepath: choosing successive life stages from a setting's lifepath lists, each granting years, skills, traits, and resource points, so history dictates capability.
vector_explanations.character.attributes.derived_attributes: Stats set by lifepaths (Will, Perception, Power, Forte, Agility, Speed) generate derived attributes — Health, Steel, Reflexes, Hesitation, and the character's Mortal Wound threshold — by formula.
vector_explanations.character.skills.skill_ranks: Skills are rated in dice and advance only by logging enough Routine, Difficult, and Challenging tests, so a character must use a skill across easy and hard tasks to raise it rather than spending XP.
vector_explanations.character.skills.skill_defaults: Beginner's Luck lets a character attempt an untrained skill using its root stat at double the Obstacle, and enough Beginner's Luck attempts eventually open that skill at a starting rating.
vector_explanations.character.identity.beliefs_instincts: Each character writes Beliefs — statements of conviction the GM is bound to challenge — and Instincts, reflexive if-then declarations that trigger automatically, making them the engine of play.
vector_explanations.character.progression.belief_xp: Pursuing, challenging, or resolving a written Belief in play earns Fate and Persona artha at the end of session, paying the player directly for acting on dramatic conviction.
vector_explanations.character.progression.instinct_xp: Acting on a written Instinct, or letting it drag the character into trouble, earns artha and can grant a free action the moment the reflex fires.
vector_explanations.character.progression.trait_vote: At session's end the whole table holds a trait vote, awarding new character traits or stripping ones the character has outgrown based on how they were actually played.
vector_explanations.social.persuasion.duel_of_wits: Formal debate uses the Duel of Wits: each side has a Body of Argument, secretly scripts verbal maneuvers (Point, Dismiss, Rebuttal, Feint) in volleys, and the winner's concessions scale to how much argument they lost.
vector_explanations.social.reputation.status_by_class: Social station bought at character burning is a rated trait reflecting class and standing, gating access and obligations and tested when the character leverages who they are.
vector_explanations.politics.factions.circles: The Circles stat is rolled to turn up a needed contact within one's social sphere; a failure invokes the Enmity Clause, conjuring a hostile acquaintance instead of the ally sought.
vector_explanations.combat.melee.fight_scripting: Fight! resolves melee by having both combatants secretly script three actions per volley — strike, block, feint, charge — then revealing and cross-referencing them action by action.
vector_explanations.combat.ranged.range_and_cover: Range and Cover scripts a positioning duel of maneuvers to close, hold, or flee across range bands, and winning the exchange earns the position to fire or to break away.
vector_explanations.combat.wounds.wound_levels: Injuries land on a ladder from Superficial through Light, Midi, Severe, and Traumatic to the character-specific Mortal Wound, each threshold set by weapon power against Forte-derived numbers.
vector_explanations.combat.wounds.death_spiral: Every wound above superficial imposes a growing dice penalty to all actions, so an injured character fights progressively worse and slides toward the Mortal Wound.
vector_explanations.economy.barter.abstract_wealth: Purchases are resolved by testing a rated Resources stat against a cost Obstacle rather than counting coin, and a failed Resources test can leave the character 'taxed', temporarily lowering the stat.
vector_explanations.magic.casting.skill_roll_casting: Sorcery is cast as an Obstacle-rated Sorcery skill test in which the caster tunes the spell's actions, distance, area, and origin, so a bigger effect raises the Obstacle and can fail outright.
vector_explanations.magic.risk.backlash_damage: Casting sorcery levies a tax: afterward the mage tests Forte against the spell's Obstacle, and failure drains Forte temporarily or permanently, physically wearing the caster down.
vector_explanations.magic.divine.faith_tests: The Faith emotional attribute powers miracles: a priest speaks a prayer and tests Faith against an Obstacle scaled to how far the miracle bends the world, with failure risking divine displeasure.

title: Torchbearer
resolution_core: d6 dice pool, 4+ successes versus Obstacle, paced by a Grind clock that inflicts conditions

vector_explanations.resolution.core_mechanic.dice_pool_succe: Tests roll a pool of d6s from ability plus help, and each die showing 4+ is a success; you need successes equal to the Obstacle, the Burning Wheel engine tuned for dungeoneering.
vector_explanations.resolution.target_numbers.success_thresh: Obstacles rate every task by the number of successes it demands, so climbing a wall, picking a lock, or resisting the grind is measured purely in hits required.
vector_explanations.resolution.target_numbers.contested_tn: Directly opposed actions resolve as Versus tests, with both parties rolling their pools and comparing successes to decide who prevails.
vector_explanations.resolution.degrees.fail_forward: A failed roll never simply stalls the delve; the GM must introduce a twist that complicates the situation or grant the intended result while inflicting a condition.
vector_explanations.resolution.modifiers.help_dice: Companions add help dice and characters bring Wises, traits, and Nature to bear, bundling the party's resources into the lead roller's pool.
vector_explanations.resolution.metacurrency.hero_points: Fate and Persona points, earned by playing to Belief, Goal, and Instinct, are spent to reroll sixes, open-end dice, or add dice when a roll must not fail.
vector_explanations.mental.conditions.condition_ladder_grind: Every action spends a turn, and each fourth turn advances the Grind, inflicting the next condition up a fixed ladder — Hungry and Thirsty, Angry, Afraid, Exhausted, Injured, Sick — that ends in death.
vector_explanations.logistics.survival.light_sources_timers: Light is a hard clock: torches, lanterns, and candles each illuminate for a set number of turns before guttering out and plunging the party into the penalties of darkness.
vector_explanations.logistics.survival.rations: Food and fresh water are tracked as inventory and consumed during camp and recovery, and exhausting them is what earns the Hungry and Thirsty condition.
vector_explanations.logistics.survival.camping_upkeep: The Camp phase demands a safe spot and a light source, then grants a limited pool of camp checks to cook, recover, or stand watch before the grind resumes.
vector_explanations.logistics.encumbrance.container_manageme: Inventory is meticulously slotted by where it is carried — hands, belt, worn, pack, and satchels — so what a character can reach in a hurry depends on how it was packed.
vector_explanations.logistics.travel.marching_order: The party sets an explicit order of march naming who bears the light and who leads, which determines who springs traps and meets ambushes first.
vector_explanations.downtime.structure.downtime_phases: Play cycles through three phases — the Adventure phase under the grind, the Camp phase for brief recovery, and the Town phase for resupply and healing — each with its own rules.
vector_explanations.downtime.structure.lifestyle_upkeep_cost: Returning to town charges a lifestyle maintenance cost in coin, and a character who cannot pay racks up conditions or must sell gear and take on debt.
vector_explanations.downtime.recovery.recovery_tests: Removing conditions is never automatic: each is cleared by a specific recovery test in camp or town, which can itself fail and leave the affliction in place.
vector_explanations.character.identity.beliefs_instincts: Every character carries a Belief, an Instinct, and a Goal on the sheet — standing convictions and reflexes the GM leans on and rewards when they drive play.
vector_explanations.character.identity.ancestry_heritage: Stock — Human, Elf, Dwarf, or Halfling — sets a character's Nature rating and its descriptors, which both fuel bonus dice and can shift as the Nature is taxed.
vector_explanations.character.skills.skill_ranks: Skills are rated in dice and advance only by logging the required pass and fail tests, so both triumphs and setbacks count toward raising an ability.
vector_explanations.character.skills.skill_defaults: A character lacking a skill may attempt it Beginner's Luck style using the relevant Nature or stat at a raised Obstacle, slowly opening the skill through repeated use.
vector_explanations.character.progression.belief_xp: Playing to your Belief, chasing your Goal, and following your Instinct are the session's rewards, paying out the Fate and Persona artha that keeps the character fueled.
vector_explanations.combat.melee.fight_scripting: Conflicts such as kill, drive off, flee, and convince are team contests where each side scripts Attack, Defend, Feint, and Maneuver in secret and reveals them action by action against a disposition pool.
vector_explanations.politics.factions.circles: The Circles ability is rolled to locate a helpful contact back in town, and failure summons an enemy or an awkward complication instead of the person sought.
vector_explanations.economy.resources.coin: Wealth is tracked as physical coin — copper, silver, and gold hauled out of dungeons — and spent in town on healing, lifestyle, and gear.
vector_explanations.equipment.durability.weapon_breakage: Weapons, tools, and gear can be broken or lost outright as the price of a failed test or a nasty twist, making equipment a genuinely depleting resource.

title: Mouse Guard Roleplaying Game
resolution_core: d6 dice pool, 4+ successes versus Obstacle; sessions split into the GM's Turn and the Players' Turn

vector_explanations.resolution.core_mechanic.dice_pool_succe: Guardmice roll a pool of d6s from an ability plus help; each die showing 4+ counts as a success, and the test passes when successes reach the Obstacle, Burning Wheel's engine at its lightest.
vector_explanations.resolution.target_numbers.success_thresh: The GM sets an Obstacle rating the number of successes a task requires, so weather, walls, and weasels are all quantified as a count of hits needed.
vector_explanations.resolution.target_numbers.contested_tn: Head-to-head actions are Versus tests in which both sides roll their pools and compare successes to determine the winner.
vector_explanations.resolution.degrees.fail_forward: When a test fails the GM must either introduce a twist that pushes the mission somewhere new or hand the mouse a condition, never a flat 'nothing happens'.
vector_explanations.resolution.degrees.success_with_cost: The GM may offer a compromise on a risky test — the mouse gets what it wanted but takes a condition or a twist in exchange — codifying success at a price.
vector_explanations.resolution.modifiers.help_dice: Patrol-mates lend help dice and a mouse can invoke Wises, gear, and Nature, gathering the patrol's combined competence into a single roll.
vector_explanations.resolution.metacurrency.hero_points: Fate and Persona points, earned by playing Belief, Goal, and Instinct, let a mouse reroll sixes, explode them, or add dice at a decisive moment.
vector_explanations.downtime.structure.downtime_phases: Each session splits into the GM's Turn, where the GM sets obstacles and drives the mission, and the Players' Turn, where mice spend earned checks to pursue their own goals and recover.
vector_explanations.downtime.recovery.recovery_tests: Conditions are shed only through dedicated recovery tests taken in the Players' Turn, which can fail and leave a mouse still Hungry, Tired, or Injured.
vector_explanations.mental.conditions.condition_ladder_grind: Hardship inflicts conditions up a fixed ladder — Hungry and Thirsty, Angry, Tired, Injured, Sick — each with its own penalty and its own required remedy.
vector_explanations.character.identity.beliefs_instincts: Every guardmouse is defined by a Belief, an Instinct, and a Goal written on the sheet, which the GM challenges and which reward the player when they steer play.
vector_explanations.character.identity.flaws_drawbacks: Rated Traits can be invoked once for a bonus die, but a player may also turn a Trait against their own mouse to earn a check, the currency that powers the Players' Turn.
vector_explanations.character.skills.skill_ranks: Skills are rated in dice and improve only by logging enough passed and failed tests, so a mouse learns from botches as much as from triumphs.
vector_explanations.character.skills.skill_defaults: A mouse attempting an unknown skill uses Beginner's Luck, rolling the relevant Nature or ability at a raised Obstacle until the skill opens through repeated use.
vector_explanations.character.progression.belief_xp: Acting on your Belief and pursuing your Goal are what earn Fate and Persona artha, tying advancement to dramatic conviction rather than to kills.
vector_explanations.character.progression.instinct_xp: Following a written Instinct, even when it lands the mouse in trouble, earns artha and can trigger a free reaction whenever the reflex applies.
vector_explanations.combat.melee.fight_scripting: Conflicts — fight, flee, convince, negotiate — are team contests where each side secretly scripts Attack, Defend, Feint, and Maneuver and reveals them action by action against a disposition pool.
vector_explanations.politics.factions.circles: The Circles ability is rolled to find the right mouse in the Territories for aid or information, with failure conjuring a rival or complication instead of the ally sought.
vector_explanations.simulation.environment.weather_effects: Weather and season are tracked and impose concrete modifiers on travel and tests, so a spring flood or a winter freeze is a mechanical obstacle in its own right.

title: Warhammer Fantasy Roleplay 4th Edition
resolution_core: d100 roll-under skill; Success Levels grade outcomes and drive opposed tests (percentile)

vector_explanations.resolution.core_mechanic.roll_under_perc: Every test rolls d100 against a Skill equal to a Characteristic plus advances; rolling equal to or under succeeds, the percentile core WFRP inherits from its earlier editions.
vector_explanations.resolution.core_mechanic.d100_criticals: Rolling a double — 11, 22, 33 and so on — under the target is a Critical hit, while a double over the target is a Fumble, so matching digits inject drama into any roll.
vector_explanations.resolution.core_mechanic.opposed_rolls: Contests are Opposed Tests where both sides roll and compare Success Levels, and the winner's margin over the loser drives the outcome and, in a fight, the damage dealt.
vector_explanations.resolution.degrees.degrees_of_success: The tens-digit difference between roll and target yields Success Levels, grading every test from marginal to spectacular and feeding directly into damage and extended tasks.
vector_explanations.resolution.modifiers.situational_modifie: The GM sets each test's difficulty with a flat modifier — Easy at +40 down to Very Hard at -30 — adjusting the target percentage before the d100 is thrown.
vector_explanations.resolution.luck.reroll_resources: Fortune points, refreshed each session from the Fate stat, are spent to reroll a failed test or add a Success Level, cushioning ordinary bad luck.
vector_explanations.resolution.luck.death_defiance_points: Fate points are a scarce reserve burned to escape otherwise certain death, permanently reducing the pool and marking a character who has cheated doom.
vector_explanations.character.attributes.rolled_stats: The twelve Characteristics are generated by rolling 2d10 and adding a species base, embracing uneven heroes, though a point-allocation option exists for those who prefer it.
vector_explanations.character.attributes.derived_attributes: Secondary values derive from Characteristic Bonuses: Wounds come from Strength, Toughness, and Willpower Bonuses, and Movement from species, all computed off the primary scores.
vector_explanations.character.skills.skill_ranks: Skills are Characteristic-linked and improved by buying advances a point at a time with experience, each advance adding one percent up to the career tier's cap.
vector_explanations.character.character_creation.random_back: Species, Class, and starting Career can be rolled randomly at creation, and players who keep their random results are rewarded with extra starting experience.
vector_explanations.character.progression.careers_system: Advancement is structured as Careers arranged in four tiers; a character buys advances within their current career, then completes it to progress or spends extra experience to change careers entirely.
vector_explanations.character.progression.experience_points: Experience points are the currency of advancement, spent to raise Characteristics, buy skill advances, and purchase Talents, with costs escalating in bands.
vector_explanations.social.reputation.status_by_class: Each Career carries a Status of Brass, Silver, or Gold at a numeric standing, which sets social rank, expected lifestyle, and how institutions treat the character.
vector_explanations.combat.melee.percentage_opposed: Melee is an Opposed Weapon Skill test: attacker and defender both roll d100, and the winner's Success Levels plus weapon damage, minus Toughness Bonus and Armour Points, determine the wound.
vector_explanations.combat.melee.momentum_based: Combatants build Advantage by winning exchanges, each point adding to further tests, so momentum snowballs until a hit lands, a test fails, or the enemy rallies and strips it away.
vector_explanations.combat.wounds.hit_points: Damage depletes a small Wounds pool derived from Characteristic Bonuses; while Wounds remain it is pure attrition, but reaching zero opens the door to Critical Wounds.
vector_explanations.combat.damage.critical_wounds: Once Wounds are gone, further hits roll on Critical Wound tables by hit location, producing specific escalating injuries — severed limbs, ruptured organs — that can maim or kill outright.
vector_explanations.combat.wounds.armor_as_soak: Armour Points by hit location, added to the Toughness Bonus, subtract directly from each hit's damage, so protection soaks wounds rather than making a target harder to strike.
vector_explanations.equipment.gear.weapon_properties_tags: Weapons carry Qualities and Flaws such as Damaging, Impale, Pummel, and Tiring that grant special rules on Criticals, doubles, or specific manoeuvres.
vector_explanations.mental.fear.fear_terror_distinction: Frightening things are tiered: Fear imposes penalties and can root a character, while the greater Terror forces immediate tests that inflict Broken conditions or outright flight.
vector_explanations.magic.acquisition.school_specialization: Wizards belong to one of the eight Colleges of Magic, each bound to a single Wind of Magic that defines its lore, spells, and the temptations it invites.
vector_explanations.magic.casting.skill_roll_casting: Casting first Channels the appropriate Wind to build Success Levels, then a casting test determines whether and how strongly the spell manifests.
vector_explanations.magic.casting.overchannel_push: A wizard may overcast, pushing a spell beyond its safe threshold for greater effect while sharply raising the odds of a Miscast.
vector_explanations.magic.risk.mishap_tables: Rolling doubles while casting triggers a Miscast, sending the wizard to Minor or Major Miscast tables of backlash, wounds, and tears in reality.
vector_explanations.magic.risk.corruption: Exposure to Chaos, dark magic, and mutation accumulates Corruption points that mark the soul's slow slide toward damnation.
vector_explanations.logistics.survival.corruption_misfortune: When Corruption crosses a threshold the character must take a Mutation test, and failure erupts into a rolled physical or mental mutation rather than a simple penalty.
vector_explanations.logistics.survival.disease: Diseases and infections run on their own tracks of Symptoms, incubation, and duration, with festering wounds and plagues that must be treated over days rather than shrugged off.

title: The One Ring (2nd Edition)
resolution_core: Roll a d12 Feat die plus d6 Success dice (per skill) versus a Target Number; Tengwar runes grade success

vector_explanations.resolution.target_numbers.static_dc: Actions roll one d12 Feat die plus a number of d6 Success dice equal to the skill, summing them against a Target Number set by the task's difficulty and usually near fourteen.
vector_explanations.resolution.degrees.degrees_of_success: Each Success die showing a 6 is a Tengwar rune; clearing the TN with one rune is a Great success and with two an Extraordinary success, unlocking stronger effects.
vector_explanations.resolution.degrees.critical_success: Rolling the Gandalf rune on the Feat die counts as an automatic success no matter the Target Number, fortune intervening in the hero's favour.
vector_explanations.resolution.degrees.critical_failure: Rolling the Eye of Sauron on the Feat die scores as a zero and can raise the Loremaster's Eye Awareness, turning a bad roll into looming peril.
vector_explanations.resolution.metacurrency.threat_pool: The Loremaster banks Eye Awareness from Eye results and shadowed lands, later spending it to deepen encounters and unleash the Enemy's servants.
vector_explanations.mental.composure.hope_dice: Heroes spend Hope from a personal pool to add their Attribute die to a roll or to activate Virtues, while gathering Shadow erodes that pool and invites despair.
vector_explanations.mental.composure.emotional_states: Crossing thresholds drops heroes into defined states — Weary from Fatigue makes low Success dice count as zero, and Miserable from Shadow risks a bout of madness.
vector_explanations.character.lifecycle.monstrous_transforma: If accumulated Shadow overwhelms a hero's Hope for good, the character is lost to the Shadow and passes out of the players' hands into the Enemy's.
vector_explanations.character.identity.ancestry_heritage: Character creation begins with a Culture — Hobbits, Bardings, Elves of Lindon, Dwarves of Durin's folk and more — that sets attribute options, starting skills, and cultural virtues.
vector_explanations.character.identity.flaws_drawbacks: A hero's Calling defines their motivation but comes bound to a Shadow weakness, the specific flaw through which the Shadow will try to claim them.
vector_explanations.character.character_creation.tags_traits: Each hero has Distinctive Features — descriptive traits like Bold or Wary — that can be invoked to gain Hope or justify an advantage when they fit the scene.
vector_explanations.character.attributes.derived_attributes: Starting Endurance and Hope, the two survival pools, are derived from Culture together with the Heart, Strength, and Wits attribute ratings chosen at creation.
vector_explanations.character.skills.skill_ranks: Skills are rated in dots from one to six, and that rating is the number of Success dice rolled, raised by spending Skill Points between adventures.
vector_explanations.character.progression.experience_points: Adventures pay out Adventure Points and Skill Points, spent to raise skills, buy Rewards and Virtues, and climb the Valour and Wisdom tracks.
vector_explanations.combat.maneuvers.stances: In combat each hero chooses a stance — Forward, Open, Defensive, or Rearward — that sets how easily they are hit and which foes they can reach.
vector_explanations.combat.wounds.hit_points: Blows deplete a hero's Endurance, and dropping to zero Endurance means being knocked out of the fight through exhaustion rather than killed.
vector_explanations.combat.wounds.lingering_injuries: A Piercing blow or a foe's exceptional success inflicts a Wound; a Wounded hero risks dying and must be treated and rest to recover over days.
vector_explanations.combat.wounds.armor_as_soak: Armour is rolled as Protection dice to reduce an incoming blow, and a helm lowers the chance that a hit becomes a dangerous Piercing Wound.
vector_explanations.equipment.gear.weapon_properties_tags: Weapons carry Damage, Edge, and Injury ratings plus a Load, so choosing a blade trades striking power and critical range against the Fatigue of carrying it.
vector_explanations.exploration.wilderness.journey_roles: On a journey each companion takes a role — Guide, Scout, Hunter, or Look-out — and makes role-specific tests as events arise along the way.
vector_explanations.logistics.travel.undertake_a_journey: Travel is resolved through the formal Journey procedure of phases and legs, converting distance into Fatigue tests and rolled events keyed to the terrain crossed.
vector_explanations.logistics.travel.weather_on_travel: Rolled weather and season modify each leg of a journey, so rain, snow, or the failing of the year presses on the travellers' Fatigue and choices.
vector_explanations.logistics.encumbrance.weight_based: Every item carries a Load value, and total Load sets a hero's Fatigue threshold, so overpacking through gear or travel is what makes a character Weary.
vector_explanations.narrative.structure.interlude_scenes: Between adventures the Fellowship Phase grants each hero an undertaking — healing, opening a sanctuary, gaining a Reward — a deliberate quiet counterpoint to peril.
vector_explanations.economy.wealth_abstraction.wealth_levels: Wealth is an abstract Standard of Living from Poor to Rich, tracked alongside Treasure, gating what a hero can afford without counting individual coins.

title: Forbidden Lands
resolution_core: d6 dice pool (attribute + skill + gear), count 6s as successes; Push to reroll (Year Zero Engine)

vector_explanations.resolution.core_mechanic.dice_pool_succe: Every roll gathers a pool of d6s from an Attribute, a Skill, and a Gear bonus, and each die showing a 6 is one success, the Year Zero Engine at the heart of the game.
vector_explanations.luck.mitigation.dice_rerolls: A character may Push a failed roll, rerolling every die that is not a 6 or a 1, but pushing trades a Willpower gain against the risk of damaging attributes and gear on the 1s.
vector_explanations.resolution.modifiers.condition_penalties: Attribute dice that roll 1s (Banes) damage the linked attribute, and survival hardships inflict the conditions Hungry, Sleepy, Thirsty, and Cold, each an ongoing penalty until relieved.
vector_explanations.resolution.metacurrency.willpower_spend: Pushing rolls and enduring hardship earns Willpower Points, a resource spent to power Talents and to fuel spells beyond their base effect.
vector_explanations.character.identity.ancestry_heritage: A character's Kin — Human, Elf, Dwarf, Halfling, Wolfkin, Goblin, or Orc — sets kin talents and starting traits at creation.
vector_explanations.character.character_creation.class_based: Profession — Druid, Fighter, Hunter, Minstrel, Peddler, Rider, Rogue, or Sorcerer — is the archetype choice, granting a profession talent and defining the character's role.
vector_explanations.character.identity.values_drives: Each adventurer has a Pride, a boastful point of self-regard that can be invoked once per session for a bonus die when the character lives up to it.
vector_explanations.character.identity.flaws_drawbacks: Each adventurer also carries a Dark Secret, a hidden shame the Gamemaster is meant to press on, complicating the character's story rather than empowering it.
vector_explanations.character.progression.perks: Talents — general, kin, and profession — are bought in ranks with experience, each granting a discrete named ability or bonus.
vector_explanations.character.progression.permanent_scars: Critical injuries rolled when a character is Broken can leave lasting scars, some imposing permanent penalties or demanding long recovery.
vector_explanations.social.reputation.fame_renown: A Reputation score climbs with notable deeds, making the adventurers recognised across the Forbidden Lands and shaping how strangers first react to them.
vector_explanations.combat.melee.fight_action: Melee is a declared Fight action from the fixed action menu, resolved with a single Melee skill roll whose successes drive weapon damage.
vector_explanations.combat.ranged.range_brackets: Distance is handled in categories — Arm's Length, Near, Short, Long, and Distant — that set the difficulty and reach of ranged attacks without measured grids.
vector_explanations.combat.initiative.card_speed: Initiative each round is set by drawing numbered initiative cards, one per combatant, so turn order reshuffles from round to round.
vector_explanations.combat.wounds.armor_as_soak: Armour is rolled as its own pool of dice when a blow lands, each 6 cancelling a point of damage, so protection soaks harm rather than deflecting the hit.
vector_explanations.combat.damage.critical_injury: Reducing an attribute to zero leaves a character Broken and rolls a critical injury on a table, some of them lethal with a countdown to death unless treated in time.
vector_explanations.equipment.durability.item_wear: Gear dice that roll 1s (Banes), and pushed rolls, degrade equipment, so weapons and tools lose their bonus and eventually break with use.
vector_explanations.equipment.durability.armor_ablation: Armour itself takes damage from mighty blows, losing armour rating over the course of a fight until it must be repaired at a smithy.
vector_explanations.equipment.crafting.monster_part_harvesti: Slain monsters can be butchered for parts using dedicated rules, and those trophies feed crafting recipes for gear and remedies.
vector_explanations.exploration.wilderness.hexcrawl_procedur: The wilderness is a hex map explored cell by cell, with each hex entered checked for its terrain, discoveries, and lurking dangers.
vector_explanations.logistics.travel.hex_travel_points: The day is divided into quarter-day stretches spent as travel or activity, and moving into a hex costs quarter-days scaled by its terrain.
vector_explanations.exploration.discovery.exploration_xp: Discovering new hexes, adventure sites, and legends grants experience, making exploration itself a primary source of advancement.
vector_explanations.logistics.survival.foraging_hunting: Food and water are gathered through Forage and Hunt tests whose yield depends on terrain and season, staving off the Hungry and Thirsty conditions.
vector_explanations.logistics.survival.rations: Rations of food and water are tracked and consumed as the party travels, and running out is what triggers the starvation and thirst penalties.
vector_explanations.magic.casting.overchannel_push: Spellcasters can Power a spell by spending extra Willpower and can Push the casting roll for more effect, both trading safety for potency.
vector_explanations.magic.risk.mishap_tables: A pushed or failed spell can trigger a magical mishap rolled on a table, unleashing wild and dangerous side effects on the caster and surroundings.
vector_explanations.simulation.building.stronghold_construct: The adventurers can claim and build a Stronghold room by room and function by function, investing resources and hirelings to develop a home base.

title: Symbaroum
resolution_core: d20 roll-under attribute; opponents modify the target and players make all the rolls

vector_explanations.resolution.core_mechanic.roll_under_attr: Every test rolls a d20 against one of eight attributes, and rolling equal to or under the modified attribute value succeeds, so a higher attribute simply passes more often.
vector_explanations.resolution.target_numbers.contested_tn: Acting against a foe modifies the target: the opponent's relevant attribute shifts your effective value up or down, so difficulty is generated by whoever resists you.
vector_explanations.resolution.modifiers.situational_modifie: Favourable or unfavourable circumstances adjust the effective attribute by a flat modifier before the d20 is rolled, tuning the odds without changing the die.
vector_explanations.meta.player_facing.player_facing_rolls: All dice sit with the players: a character rolls their Defense to resist an enemy's attack and rolls to persuade or perceive, so the players' rolls decide every exchange.
vector_explanations.meta.player_facing.gm_never_rolls: The Game Master never rolls dice at all, instead setting monster attributes as static values that the players test against on their own turns and in defense.
vector_explanations.character.attributes.standard_array: At creation a character distributes a fixed set of values across the eight attributes, guaranteeing a balanced spread of strengths and weaknesses.
vector_explanations.character.progression.experience_points: Advancement spends experience on Abilities, Powers, and traits, each raised across the Novice, Adept, and Master tiers as points are invested.
vector_explanations.character.lifecycle.monstrous_transforma: A character whose permanent corruption reaches their threshold becomes Thoroughly Corrupt, a blight-marked abomination lost to the player and taken over by the Game Master.
vector_explanations.combat.initiative.dexterity_based: Turn order in combat is set by the Quick attribute, with the quickest combatants acting first in a fixed order each round.
vector_explanations.combat.action_economy.minor_major_action: Each turn a character takes one active action plus a free action and movement, with reactions like defending available out of turn, structuring what can be done per round.
vector_explanations.combat.wounds.hit_points: Damage depletes Toughness, a hit-point pool derived from the Strong attribute, and reaching zero drops the character dying.
vector_explanations.combat.wounds.massive_damage: A single hit exceeding the Pain Threshold, half of Strong, forces a Strong test or the character is knocked from their feet and loses their next turn.
vector_explanations.combat.wounds.armor_as_soak: Armour is rolled as protection dice that subtract from each incoming blow, so heavier armour soaks more damage at the cost of impeding mystical powers.
vector_explanations.equipment.gear.weapon_properties_tags: Weapons and armour carry qualities such as Deep Impact, Precise, or Flexible that grant special rules beyond their base damage or protection.
vector_explanations.equipment.gear.artifact_rules: Artifacts are singular, powerful items, often thistle-touched, whose use can levy corruption, tying the strongest gear to the game's central risk.
vector_explanations.magic.acquisition.school_specialization: Mystics commit to a tradition — Theurgy, Witchcraft, Wizardry, Sorcery, Staff Magic, or Trollsinging — that frames which powers they may learn and how their corruption is understood.
vector_explanations.magic.casting.ritual_casting: Beyond combat Powers, Mystics perform Rituals — lengthy ceremonies like Bind Thrall or Exorcise — that require time and components rather than a single action.
vector_explanations.magic.risk.corruption: Channelling mystical powers inflicts temporary corruption, and casting from tainted ground or overreaching accrues it faster, marking magic as inherently defiling.
vector_explanations.logistics.survival.corruption_track: Corruption fills a track split between temporary corruption that fades with rest and permanent corruption that never does, with thresholds that steadily blight the character.
vector_explanations.downtime.recovery.natural_healing_rates: Lost Toughness returns through rest at fixed rates, with medical care or healing powers speeding recovery between fights.

title: Legend of the Five Rings 5th Edition
resolution_core: Roll-and-keep custom dice (Ring + Skill dice); keep up to Ring, read success, opportunity, and strife symbols

vector_explanations.resolution.core_mechanic.roll_and_keep: A check rolls a pool of Ring dice (six-sided) and Skill dice (twelve-sided), then keeps dice up to the governing Ring, reading kept symbols against a target number of successes.
vector_explanations.resolution.core_mechanic.symbol_dice: The custom dice show bespoke faces — success, explosive success, opportunity, and strife — read in combination rather than summed as numbers.
vector_explanations.resolution.core_mechanic.exploding_dice: Certain rolled symbols are explosive successes that let the die be rerolled and added, so a lucky check can open-end well beyond its base pool.
vector_explanations.resolution.degrees.dual_axis_outcomes: Every check reads two independent axes: successes decide whether the task is achieved, while opportunity is a separate surplus spent on side benefits like reducing strife or adding effects.
vector_explanations.resolution.metacurrency.hero_points: Void points, drawn from a small per-scene pool tied to the Void Ring, are spent to keep an extra die, add a success, or shrug off strife at a pivotal moment.
vector_explanations.character.skills.attribute_skill_pairing: Each action pairs a Skill with one of five Rings chosen as the approach — attacking with Fire versus Water reshapes the same skill — so the elemental stance frames every roll.
vector_explanations.mental.stress.stress_track: Kept dice can carry Strife that accumulates on a track, and exceeding your Composure forces an Unmasking, a loss of poise that vents the character's true feelings.
vector_explanations.character.identity.values_drives: Every samurai is torn between giri, their duty, and ninjō, their personal desire, and acting on the tension between the two drives play and earns advancement.
vector_explanations.character.identity.honor_glory_status: Public standing is tracked as three separate scores — Honor for personal integrity, Glory for renown, and Status for official rank — each shifting with conduct.
vector_explanations.social.reputation.status_by_class: Rigid samurai caste sets what conduct is expected and how each social tier must be addressed, so Status governs the etiquette a character must observe or dare to offend.
vector_explanations.character.character_creation.questionnai: Characters are built by answering the Twenty Questions, a guided in-fiction questionnaire whose answers set clan, family, school, relationships, and giri and ninjō.
vector_explanations.character.identity.splat_membership: Clan membership — Crab, Crane, Dragon, Lion, Phoenix, Scorpion, or Unicorn — is the defining allegiance, colouring abilities, reputation, and political position.
vector_explanations.character.character_creation.class_based: A character's School — bushi, shugenja, courtier, monk, or shinobi — grants a School Ability and a curriculum of techniques that define their competencies.
vector_explanations.character.progression.experience_points: Experience points are spent to raise Rings and Skills and to learn techniques, with a School curriculum guiding cheaper advancement toward higher rank.
vector_explanations.combat.maneuvers.martial_techniques: Techniques — kata, kihō, shūji, invocations, rituals, and ninjutsu — are learned as a named list, each unlocking a specific special effect in play.
vector_explanations.combat.wounds.hit_points: Harm accrues as Fatigue against an Endurance limit, and being overwhelmed by fatigue incapacitates the character rather than tracking a large hit-point pool.
vector_explanations.combat.damage.critical_injury: Critical strikes inflict rated wounds whose severity rises toward death, imposing penalties and threatening a downed or dying samurai.
vector_explanations.combat.wounds.armor_as_soak: Armour grants physical resistance that reduces the deadliness of blows and softens critical strikes rather than making a samurai harder to hit.
vector_explanations.equipment.gear.weapon_properties_tags: Weapons carry qualities such as Razor-Edged, Ceremonial, or Sacred that grant special effects, many unlocked by spending opportunity from the roll.
vector_explanations.magic.divine.spirit_bargaining: Shugenja work magic by petitioning the elemental kami through Invocations, entreating the spirits of Air, Earth, Fire, Water, and Void to act on their behalf.
vector_explanations.magic.casting.ritual_casting: Longer magics — rituals and the forbidden blood sorcery of mahō — are cast as extended ceremonies distinct from a single battlefield invocation.

title: 7th Sea (2nd Edition)
resolution_core: d10 dice pool (Trait + Skill) grouped into sets of 10+ to make Raises, spent on actions and to avoid Consequences

vector_explanations.resolution.core_mechanic.dice_pool_succe: A check rolls a pool of d10s equal to a Trait plus a Skill, then the player groups those dice into sets summing to ten or more, and each completed set forms one Raise.
vector_explanations.resolution.degrees.raises: Raises are the currency of action: each Raise made buys one thing accomplished during the sequence — a blow landed, a Consequence avoided, an Opportunity seized.
vector_explanations.resolution.degrees.success_with_cost: When a Risk is set, the GM names Consequences the scene will inflict unless bought off with Raises, so spending Raises elsewhere means accepting the harm.
vector_explanations.resolution.metacurrency.hero_points: Heroes earn Hero Points, chiefly by activating their Hubris to their own detriment, and spend them for bonus dice, extra activations, or to power Advantages.
vector_explanations.resolution.metacurrency.threat_pool: The GM banks Danger Points from the Heroes' choices and villainy afoot, spending them to buy Villain Raises, spring reinforcements, and heighten the odds.
vector_explanations.character.attributes.point_buy: At creation a Hero distributes points to set the five Traits and to raise Skills, trading breadth of competence against a few standout ratings.
vector_explanations.character.skills.attribute_skill_pairing: Every roll pairs one of the five Traits — Brawn, Finesse, Resolve, Wits, Panache — with the Skill that fits the action, so context picks the Trait each time.
vector_explanations.character.identity.values_drives: Each Hero has a Virtue that can be activated once per session for a strong narrative bonus when the Hero lives up to it.
vector_explanations.character.identity.flaws_drawbacks: Each Hero also has a Hubris, a flaw the player can activate to complicate their own life in exchange for a Hero Point.
vector_explanations.character.identity.backgrounds: Character creation is driven by Backgrounds, each granting Advantages, a Quirk that earns Hero Points when played, and a starting Story.
vector_explanations.character.progression.perks: Advantages are purchased perks — from Rich and Linguist to combat gambits — each a discrete named ability bought with points or earned through play.
vector_explanations.campaign.progression.story_xp_triggers: Advancement happens entirely through Stories: a Hero defines a personal quest as steps toward a reward, and completing the steps grants the raise or Advantage named as its prize.
vector_explanations.combat.action_economy.action_point_pools: In an Action Sequence Heroes spend their Raises as a budget of actions across the round, each Raise buying a move, an attack, or an interaction in an initiative built from Raises.
vector_explanations.combat.maneuvers.martial_techniques: Duelists learn Style maneuvers — Slash, Lunge, Riposte, Feint, Parry — as named techniques that convert Raises into signature swashbuckling effects.
vector_explanations.combat.wounds.wound_levels: Harm accrues as Wounds, and every fourth Wound becomes a Dramatic Wound on a short ladder that ends in the Hero being helpless or dying.
vector_explanations.combat.wounds.death_spiral: Each Dramatic Wound makes a Hero easier to strike and to burden with effects, letting Villains spend fewer Raises against them, so the injured slide toward defeat.
vector_explanations.magic.acquisition.bloodline_innate: Sorcery runs in noble bloodlines, each nation's magic — Sorte, Porté, Glamour, and the rest — inherited rather than studied and carrying its own rules and prices.
vector_explanations.politics.factions.patron_mechanics: Heroes may belong to Secret Societies such as the Rilasciare or Die Kreuzritter, patrons that supply missions, contacts, and resources in exchange for service.
vector_explanations.narrative.tone.pulp_conventions: The rules enforce swashbuckling pulp: Heroes cannot be killed by ordinary mooks, sweep aside Brute Squads, and escape cliffhangers, keeping the tone daring and cinematic.
vector_explanations.narrative.tone.genre_emulation_rules: The system deliberately emulates cape-and-sword fiction, framing Heroes as always competent and dramatic while Villains scheme and monologue rather than simply winning.

title: Ars Magica 5th Edition
resolution_core: d10 (stress or simple) + Characteristic + Ability versus an Ease Factor; Hermetic magic totals Technique + Form

vector_explanations.resolution.core_mechanic.exploding_dice: Most tests use a stress die: a d10 that on a 1 is rolled again and the result doubled, cascading upward, while a 0 threatens a botch, so tense rolls can soar or collapse.
vector_explanations.resolution.target_numbers.static_dc: Ordinary tasks resolve against an Ease Factor, a fixed target that the total of die plus Characteristic plus Ability must equal or exceed.
vector_explanations.resolution.degrees.critical_failure: Rolling a 0 on a stress die triggers botch dice, and each additional 0 among them deepens the disaster, from a fumbled spell to a Twilight-inducing catastrophe.
vector_explanations.resolution.degrees.margin_of_success: Combat and lab work read the margin: attack total minus defence total is the Advantage added to damage, and a Lab Total's excess over a spell level speeds its invention.
vector_explanations.resolution.metacurrency.hero_points: Confidence points, a small personal reserve, are spent to add to a roll or reroll, letting a magus push a critical casting or a companion's daring deed.
vector_explanations.character.attributes.point_buy: The seven Characteristics, rated from -3 to +3, are bought from a points budget with higher scores costing steeply more.
vector_explanations.character.skills.skill_ranks: Abilities are rated in scores bought with experience against rising thresholds, so each higher rank of a skill demands more accumulated study.
vector_explanations.character.character_creation.lifepath: Characters are built across life stages — early childhood, later life, and for magi a fifteen-year apprenticeship — each granting seasons of experience to spend on Abilities and Arts.
vector_explanations.character.identity.flaws_drawbacks: Every character balances Virtues against Flaws point for point, taking rated hindrances and story problems to pay for special powers and Hermetic gifts.
vector_explanations.character.identity.splat_membership: A magus belongs to one of the Houses of the Order of Hermes — Bonisagus, Tremere, Flambeau, and the rest — each granting distinctive powers, obligations, and politics.
vector_explanations.character.lifecycle.troupe_play: Each player keeps a stable of characters — a powerful magus, a skilled companion, and expendable grogs — and plays whichever the session's story calls for.
vector_explanations.character.lifecycle.aging: As years pass magi make aging rolls that accrue aging points and Decrepitude, gradually weakening Characteristics until death, staved off only by Longevity Rituals.
vector_explanations.character.progression.experience_points: Characters gain experience each season from study, practice, and exposure, spending it to raise Abilities and the magical Arts.
vector_explanations.combat.melee.dice_rolls: An attack is a single roll of Attack total against the defender's Defence total, the difference deciding whether and how hard the blow lands.
vector_explanations.combat.wounds.wound_levels: Injuries fall onto a ladder of wound levels — Light, Medium, Heavy, Incapacitated, Dead — rather than a hit-point pool, each step a discrete state.
vector_explanations.combat.wounds.death_spiral: Each wound level imposes a penalty to all rolls, so a hurt character fights and casts progressively worse and is likelier to take the next, graver wound.
vector_explanations.combat.wounds.armor_as_soak: Armour adds to a character's Soak, which subtracts from each hit's damage before a wound level is assigned, so protection absorbs harm rather than avoiding it.
vector_explanations.logistics.survival.exhaustion: Exertion and spontaneous casting spend Fatigue levels down a ladder — Fresh, Winded, Weary, Tired, Dazed, Unconscious — each imposing penalties until rest restores them.
vector_explanations.magic.structure.technique_form_combos: Hermetic magic is built by pairing one of five Techniques (Creo, Intellego, Muto, Perdo, Rego) with one of ten Forms (Ignem, Corpus, Terram and the rest), so a small grid of Arts generates all of magic.
vector_explanations.magic.casting.freeform_improvised: Spontaneous magic lets a magus improvise any effect within their Arts on the spot, dividing the casting total to price the invented spell, at the cost of Fatigue.
vector_explanations.magic.casting.skill_roll_casting: Casting sums Technique plus Form plus Stamina plus a die against a spell's level or Ease Factor, so spells can fail, fall short, or exceed their intended power.
vector_explanations.magic.casting.counterspelling: Certamen resolves magical duels as opposed Technique-and-Form contests, and magi can likewise oppose or counter each other's spells with mastered techniques.
vector_explanations.magic.casting.group_cooperative_casting: Rituals like Wizard's Communion let multiple magi pool their efforts, combining casting totals to reach magnitudes none could achieve alone.
vector_explanations.magic.structure.sympathetic_links: Reaching a distant target requires an Arcane Connection — hair, a possession, a true image — whose strength governs the range and permanence of the magic.
vector_explanations.magic.structure.power_sites_auras: Magic is stronger or weaker by place: magical, divine, faerie, and infernal auras add to or subtract from casting totals, making location strategic.
vector_explanations.magic.structure.enchantment_item_craftin: Permanent magic is invested into items through lab enchantment — charged items, lesser devices, and invested talismans — each demanding seasons of work and raw vis.
vector_explanations.magic.acquisition.mentor_apprenticeship: Hermetic magic is passed from master to apprentice over a fifteen-year apprenticeship, and teaching an apprentice is itself a long lab commitment.
vector_explanations.magic.acquisition.spell_research: Magi invent new formulaic spells and refine effects through lab research, their Lab Total against the spell level setting how many seasons the work takes.
vector_explanations.magic.acquisition.spellbook_study: Spells and Arts are learned from the covenant's library of summae and tomes, and lab texts let a magus reconstruct another's spells, making books precious treasure.
vector_explanations.magic.risk.doom_accumulation: Exposure to powerful magic accrues Warping points, and enough of them plunge a magus into Twilight, an episode that can enlighten, scar, or claim them forever.
vector_explanations.downtime.projects.seasonal_activities: Time between adventures is measured in seasons, and each season is committed wholesale to one pursuit — study, invention, enchantment, or teaching.
vector_explanations.logistics.downtime.laboratory_research: A magus's laboratory has its own rated qualities, and its Lab Total drives every season of research, enchantment, and longevity work performed there.
vector_explanations.logistics.maintenance.covenant_upkeep: The magi share a Covenant whose vis sources, library, and grogs must be maintained season to season, with neglect and rivalry steadily eroding its resources.
vector_explanations.campaign.management.campaign_calendars: Play tracks an explicit seasonal calendar across years and decades, so the covenant's history, aging, and long projects all march along the same dated timeline.

title: Mage: The Ascension 20th Anniversary Edition
resolution_core: d10 dice pool, count successes at a per-die difficulty; magic rolls Arete by Sphere (Storyteller)

vector_explanations.resolution.core_mechanic.dice_pool_succe: Actions roll a pool of d10s equal to Attribute plus Ability, and each die meeting the difficulty is a success, with more successes meaning a better result, the Storyteller System core.
vector_explanations.resolution.target_numbers.variable_tn: The number to beat is a difficulty set per die from 3 to 9 by task and, for magic, by how far the effect strains reality, so harder feats raise the target rather than the count needed.
vector_explanations.resolution.degrees.degrees_of_success: The number of successes grades the outcome, from one for a marginal result to five for a spectacular one, and extra successes on a spell extend its duration, damage, or scope.
vector_explanations.resolution.degrees.critical_failure: Rolling 1s cancels successes, and a roll that comes up with 1s and no successes is a botch, a dramatic failure that goes actively wrong.
vector_explanations.resolution.metacurrency.willpower_spend: A point of Willpower can be spent to gain one automatic success or to grip self-control, a scarce reserve central to pushing through crises.
vector_explanations.character.attributes.point_buy: Attributes are dot-rated and set by prioritising the Physical, Social, and Mental categories, then spending a fixed allotment of dots within that priority.
vector_explanations.character.skills.skill_ranks: Abilities — Talents, Skills, and Knowledges — are bought as dots, and raising a rating with experience costs more at each higher dot.
vector_explanations.character.identity.splat_membership: A mage belongs to one of the nine Traditions — Order of Hermes, Verbena, Cult of Ecstasy, and the rest — which shapes paradigm, allies, and affinity Sphere.
vector_explanations.character.identity.nature_demeanor: Each character has a Nature, the true inner archetype, and a Demeanor, the mask shown to the world, with mechanical reward tied to acting on the Nature.
vector_explanations.character.identity.backgrounds: Backgrounds like Avatar, Arcane, Resources, and Allies are dot-rated assets bought at creation that connect the mage to the setting.
vector_explanations.character.progression.experience_points: Experience points advance the character, spent to raise Attributes, Abilities, Spheres, and above all Arete, with steep costs for the magical ratings.
vector_explanations.mental.composure.willpower_recovery: Willpower is regained by acting in accordance with one's Nature archetype, so expressing who the character truly is refuels their resolve.
vector_explanations.mental.composure.willpower_tests: Resisting mind control, terror, or supernatural coercion calls for a Willpower roll, a test distinct from spending the trait outright.
vector_explanations.combat.initiative.dexterity_based: Initiative each turn is a d10 added to Dexterity plus Wits, the fastest reflexes acting first in the round.
vector_explanations.combat.melee.dice_pools: Attacks roll a dice pool of Attribute plus Ability against a difficulty, counting successes to determine whether and how well the blow connects.
vector_explanations.combat.wounds.wound_levels: Health is a ladder of levels — Bruised, Hurt, Wounded, Mauled, Crippled, Incapacitated — rather than a hit-point pool, each step a discrete state of injury.
vector_explanations.combat.wounds.death_spiral: Lower health levels impose escalating dice penalties on all actions, so the badly hurt act worse and spiral toward incapacitation.
vector_explanations.combat.wounds.armor_as_soak: Damage is soaked with a Stamina and armour roll whose successes cancel damage dice before health levels are marked off.
vector_explanations.magic.structure.schools_of_magic: Magic is organised into nine Spheres — Correspondence, Entropy, Forces, Life, Matter, Mind, Prime, Spirit, Time — and a mage's rating in each governs what they can affect.
vector_explanations.magic.structure.belief_paradigms: What a mage can do and how is defined by their Paradigm, practices, and Instruments, so different mages achieve the same effect through wholly different rituals.
vector_explanations.magic.casting.freeform_improvised: Rather than a fixed spell list, a mage improvises effects by combining Spheres at their rating, the Storyteller pricing each on the fly by scope and difficulty.
vector_explanations.magic.casting.skill_roll_casting: Casting rolls Arete, the measure of enlightenment, against a difficulty set by the effect, with successes determining whether and how strongly the magic manifests.
vector_explanations.magic.casting.mana_points: Quintessence, the raw stuff of magic, is a pool spent to lower casting difficulty or empower effects, replenished from Nodes, Tass, and the Prime Sphere.
vector_explanations.magic.casting.ritual_casting: Ambitious effects are cast as extended rituals over minutes, hours, or longer, accumulating successes across many rolls instead of a single action.
vector_explanations.magic.casting.group_cooperative_casting: Multiple mages can combine in group rituals, pooling Arete and Spheres to achieve magic beyond any single caster's reach.
vector_explanations.magic.risk.paradox: Working magic that flouts consensual reality, especially vulgar magic seen by Sleepers, builds Paradox that rebounds as backlash, Paradox flaws, or a maddening Quiet.
vector_explanations.magic.risk.unwanted_attention: Blatant, vulgar magic draws notice — from Sleeper witnesses to Technocracy agents and hostile spirits — making discretion a survival skill.
vector_explanations.simulation.astral.planar_travel: With the Spirit Sphere a mage can step through the Gauntlet into the Umbra, the spirit worlds, which run by their own laws and demand their own navigation.

title: Exalted 3rd Edition
resolution_core: d10 dice pool, 7+ successes with 10s counting double; difficulty = successes needed (Storyteller-derived)

vector_explanations.resolution.core_mechanic.dice_pool_succe: Actions roll a pool of d10s from Attribute plus Ability, each 7 or higher a success and each 10 counting as two, the Storyteller-descended engine at Exalted's heart.
vector_explanations.resolution.target_numbers.success_thresh: Difficulty is the number of successes a task requires, so the challenge is measured in hits needed rather than a single target roll.
vector_explanations.resolution.degrees.degrees_of_success: Threshold successes above the difficulty matter, adding raw damage to an attack or extra effect to a feat, so overshooting the target is rewarded.
vector_explanations.resolution.degrees.critical_failure: A roll that shows 1s and no successes is a botch, an emphatic failure the Storyteller turns against the character.
vector_explanations.resolution.metacurrency.willpower_spend: Willpower can be spent for a guaranteed success on a roll or to resist unwanted influence, a scarce reserve of resolve.
vector_explanations.character.attributes.point_buy: Attributes and Abilities are dot-rated and set by prioritising categories and spending a fixed allotment of dots, with bonus and experience points fine-tuning the build.
vector_explanations.character.skills.skill_ranks: Abilities are rated in dots that set the base dice pool, and raising them with experience costs more at each higher rank.
vector_explanations.character.character_creation.incarnation: A Solar bears the Exaltation of an ancient hero, and the deeds and enemies of those prior incarnations echo into the new bearer's story.
vector_explanations.character.identity.splat_membership: Each Solar belongs to a Caste — Dawn, Zenith, Twilight, Night, or Eclipse — that favours certain Abilities, anima powers, and social roles.
vector_explanations.character.progression.perks: Charms are hundreds of purchased supernatural powers arranged in Ability trees, and Excellencies add dice to rolls up to a cap set by the governing Attribute plus Ability.
vector_explanations.character.progression.experience_points: Advancement spends experience, split into general and Solar experience, to raise traits and buy Charms, with in-Caste and favoured powers discounted.
vector_explanations.mental.composure.willpower_recovery: Willpower is regained through restful sleep and by playing to Intimacies, and dramatic stunts refund both Willpower and motes.
vector_explanations.mental.composure.intimacies_leverage: Rated Intimacies — Defining, Major, and Minor loves, hatreds, and principles — are the levers of social influence, lowering Resolve when an argument aligns with them.
vector_explanations.mental.composure.frenzy_loss_of_control: Acting against a Solar's nature builds Limit, and a full Limit track triggers a Limit Break, the Great Curse seizing the character into a rules-driven emotional eruption.
vector_explanations.social.structure.social_defenses: Social attacks must beat a target's Resolve or, when deceit is involved, their Guile, static defences that can be raised by invoking one's own Intimacies and virtues.
vector_explanations.social.structure.social_combat_tracks: Influence is its own subsystem of instill, inspire, persuade, and threaten rolls that build, exploit, and reshape a target's Intimacies to move them toward a decision.
vector_explanations.combat.maneuvers.combat_stunts: Describing a vivid, cinematic action earns a one-, two-, or three-die stunt bonus and refunds Willpower or motes, mechanically rewarding flourish.
vector_explanations.combat.initiative.initiative_momentum: Combat opens with a Join Battle roll and runs on an Initiative score combatants fight to seize and hold, acting in Initiative order that shifts every round.
vector_explanations.combat.melee.momentum_based: Withering attacks strip an enemy's Initiative and add it to your own, and once ahead you unleash a decisive attack that spends that stored Initiative as damage before resetting to base.
vector_explanations.combat.wounds.wound_levels: Health is a short track of levels with worsening wound penalties rather than a large pool, each filled box a step toward incapacitation.
vector_explanations.combat.wounds.death_spiral: Marked health levels impose escalating dice penalties on all actions, so a wounded Exalt fights worse and slides toward being taken out.
vector_explanations.combat.wounds.armor_as_soak: Armour and Stamina provide Soak that subtracts from withering damage, while Hardness sets a floor a decisive attack must exceed to bite.
vector_explanations.magic.casting.mana_points: Charms are fuelled by motes of Essence drawn from Personal and Peripheral pools, which recover over time and through stunts and channels.
vector_explanations.magic.casting.ritual_casting: Sorcery is worked by shaping Essence over one or more rounds to cast spells, a distinct ritual subsystem layered atop Charms.
vector_explanations.magic.casting.counterspelling: Sorcerers can weave countermagic to unravel or block an opponent's spell, an opposed contest of shaped Essence.
vector_explanations.magic.structure.spell_levels: Sorcery is tiered into Circles — Terrestrial, Celestial, and Solar — that gate which spells an Exalt can learn and cast.
vector_explanations.magic.risk.unwanted_attention: Spending Peripheral Essence lights a character's anima banner brighter and brighter, eventually blazing into a visible display that marks them unmistakably as an Exalt.
vector_explanations.equipment.gear.artifact_rules: Artifacts are singular magical treasures with their own Evocation powers, drawn on by attuned wielders and central to a Solar's legend.
vector_explanations.equipment.gear.magic_item_attunement: Using an artifact requires attuning to it by committing motes of Essence, so each committed relic reduces the mote pool available for Charms.

title: Paranoia
resolution_core: d20 against a Gamemaster-set target with the rules kept secret from players; The Computer's word is final

vector_explanations.resolution.core_mechanic.d20_vs_dc: Task resolution is built on a d20 roll against a target the Gamemaster sets from the acting citizen's skills and stats, though the true rules stay hidden behind the GM's screen.
vector_explanations.meta.player_facing.asymmetric_informatio: The rulebook is Ultraviolet clearance: players are forbidden to read the rules, so the Gamemaster alone knows the mechanics and citizens act on deliberate ignorance.
vector_explanations.narrative.authority.final_authority_veto: The Computer is always right and the Gamemaster speaks for it, holding absolute authority to overrule, reinterpret, or terminate on a whim.
vector_explanations.narrative.tone.comedy_mechanics: The whole system is engineered for farce, with expendable clones, backfiring gadgets, and absurd bureaucracy turning constant lethal failure into comedy.
vector_explanations.social.deception.hidden_roles: Every Troubleshooter secretly belongs to a subversive secret society whose agenda they must advance while hiding their membership from the team.
vector_explanations.deduction.hidden_roles.secret_identities: Each citizen conceals treasonous secrets — a secret-society tie and an unregistered mutation — that mean termination if ever exposed to The Computer.
vector_explanations.social.deception.misinformation_planting: Play turns on framing teammates, planting false evidence, and denouncing rivals for treason to The Computer before they do the same to you.
vector_explanations.social.deception.bluff_challenges: Accusations of treason can be levelled and denied at any moment, and whether a claim sticks turns on evidence, bluff, and who The Computer chooses to believe.
vector_explanations.magic.psionics.wild_talents: Every clone possesses one mutant power, an illegal knack from telepathy to rubbery bones that is itself treason and must be used in secret.
vector_explanations.campaign.continuity.replacement_characte: Each citizen is issued a batch of six numbered clones, and a terminated Troubleshooter is simply replaced by the next clone, delivered to continue the mission.
vector_explanations.character.lifecycle.death_and_dying: Death is frequent, casual, and often self-inflicted, and the rules treat the demise of a clone as a routine, replaceable event rather than a tragedy.
vector_explanations.character.identity.splat_membership: Beyond secret societies, each Troubleshooter is assigned to a Service Group — from Internal Security to Technical Services — that dictates official duties and privileges.
vector_explanations.character.skills.skill_ranks: Citizens have rated skills and attributes that feed the resolution roll, though a Troubleshooter is rarely told their own exact numbers.
vector_explanations.combat.wounds.hit_points: Damage is tracked against a citizen's health, but combat is swift and lethal, so a serious hit often simply ends the current clone.
vector_explanations.social.reputation.status_by_class: Society is stratified by security-clearance colours from INFRARED up to ULTRAVIOLET, and access, equipment, and even walkable corridors are gated by rank, with acting above your clearance counting as treason.
vector_explanations.politics.factions.servility_authority: Citizens owe absolute obedience to The Computer, and orders that cut against survival must still be followed or reported, making loyalty itself a trap.
vector_explanations.politics.factions.cabal_influence: Secret societies quietly work their influence through Alpha Complex, handing members missions and leverage that pull against their official duties.
vector_explanations.politics.intrigue.blackmail_leverage: Compromising evidence of a teammate's mutations, society ties, or blunders is hoarded as leverage, spent to coerce them or sold to Internal Security.
vector_explanations.social.intimidation.coercion_threats: Superiors and The Computer extract compliance through threats of termination, and citizens wield the same coercion against each other to force cooperation.
vector_explanations.equipment.durability.weapon_jamming: Experimental R&D equipment is unreliable by design, jamming, misfiring, and malfunctioning at the worst possible moments as a running source of catastrophe.
